topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink swam before my eyes, a stark, unforgiving slash across the top of the graded paper. A C-minus. I stared at the number, my breath catching in a shallow, ragged gasp. My hands, which moments before had been steady enough to write a flawless essay, began to tremble uncontrollably. I slowly lowered the paper, the sound of the rustling cheap paper amplifying in the sudden silence of my cramped dorm room. It felt as if the floor had tilted beneath me. Every muscle in my jaw tightened until it ached, and a cold, clammy sweat prickled the back of my neck. *This isn't right. I know I understood the material. I spent weeks on this.* I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to force the image of the correct answers back into my mind, but all I could see was that damning, low grade.

[story 2]

"A D-?" I whispered the words, the sound thin and reedy in the quiet library. My entire body seemed to seize up. I picked up the feedback sheet, the paper feeling suddenly heavy, weighted with failure. My fingers fumbled at the typed comments, tracing the criticisms with a fingertip, feeling a strange, prickly heat bloom across my skin. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning in protest, and let out a shaky exhale that sounded more like a choked sob. My vision tunneled slightly at the edges, and I gripped the edge of the table so tightly my knuckles bleached white. The world outside the circle of my immediate distress seemed muted, distant, as if I were submerged underwater.

[story 3]

When Professor Albright announced the results, the air in the lecture hall seemed to thicken, turning viscous and suffocating. My stomach lurched violently, a sickening drop in my core that made me instinctively clutch the strap of my backpack. I couldn't look at the board; I focused instead on the small pile of papers on my desk, and the color drained from my face, leaving a sickly, pale expanse. A low, involuntary moan escaped my throat, quickly swallowed by the cavernous room. My legs felt suddenly weak, as if they had turned to water. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal against linoleum sounding deafeningly loud, and I just wanted to disappear into the nearest shadow.
